title: Te Kōrero o te Awa: The Living Story of Our River / Te Kōrero o te Awa: He Kōrero Ora mō Tō Tātou Awa
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: Students will explore the cultural significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream) through the lens of Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau (traditional stories), connecting language learning with the living history of our local waterway and its role in traditional food gathering, navigation, and spiritual practices
learningObjectives: Identify and use 10-15 Māori kupu (words) related to waterways and traditional practices in meaningful sentences
Recount the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using appropriate sentence structures and vocabulary
Create a simple pepeha (introduction) that acknowledges their connection to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Participate in a basic mihi whakatau (welcome ceremony) using appropriate greetings and responses

activities.title: He Hīkoi ki te Awa: Journey to the Stream
activities.description: Guided visit to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku where students observe and document features using Māori vocabulary (awa, wai, repo, etc.) while listening to local kaumātua share the stream's history and significance

activities.title: He Kōrero Tuku Iho: Stories of Our Ancestors
activities.description: Interactive storytelling session where students learn the pūrākau of how the stream got its name and its importance to Ngāti Kahungunu, followed by role-playing key events from the story

activities.title: Te Reo o te Awa: Language of the Waterway
activities.description: Vocabulary building workshop using flashcards and games focused on water-related terms, environmental concepts, and traditional practices connected to the awa

activities.title: Taku Pepeha, Taku Awa: My Connection, My River
activities.description: Students create and practice delivering a personal pepeha that includes acknowledgment of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, using sentence structures and vocabulary learned in class

activities.title: He Mihi Whakatau: Welcoming to Our Place
activities.description: Practice and participation in a simulated mihi whakatau where students use appropriate greetings, responses, and waiata to welcome visitors to the college near the awa

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu (specifically Aunty Hine Smith who knows the stream's history)
Te Aka Māori Dictionary online resource (www.maoridictionary.co.nz)
Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated website and cultural resources
'Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: He Pūrākau' booklet from college archives

tasks: Record a 1-minute video introducing yourself with your pepeha including the awa
Create a visual vocabulary poster with 10 water-related Māori words and illustrations
Participate in a group retelling of the stream's pūrākau using key vocabulary
Complete a listening comprehension exercise based on the kaumātua's storytelling

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Level 4: Use basic Māori patterns and structures to communicate about immediate and familiar environments
Te Reo Māori: Language - Level 4: Understand how Māori language expresses cultural meanings
Social Sciences: Identity, Culture, and Organization - Level 4: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
The Arts: Understanding the Arts in Context - Level 4: Investigate the purpose of objects and images from past and present cultures
